text:
St Mary's Hospital 

Institute of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Patel  
St Mary's Hospital  
Institute of Neurology, St Mary's Hospital, London W2 1NY  

Dear Dr Patel,  

Sophie Ahmed, DOB: 12-11-1994, Hospital No: H918204 NHS No. 6035812479  
Flat 12, 4 Kings Wharf, London, W2 5AL 

Medication: 

Levetiracetam 1 g twice daily. Tolerated without mood change or somnolence. Patient uses pill box and sets phone alarms aligned to shift pattern.

Thank you for reviewing this rotating shift worker who undertakes nights and reports a markedly disrupted circadian rhythm with variable sleep onset and duration. She has known generalised epilepsy with typical myoclonic jerks on waking and rare generalised tonic–clonic seizures. Despite adherence to levetiracetam, she continues to have brief clusters of morning myoclonus and has experienced a single generalised tonic–clonic seizure in the last two months after three consecutive night shifts. Overall, she reports events every week, usually brief myoclonic jerks on awakening after short or fragmented sleep. There are no focal warnings. Partners have not observed asymmetry, tongue biting, or prolonged post-ictal confusion with the myoclonic events; recovery is rapid within minutes. 

She identifies clear precipitants: sleep deprivation related to rota changes, consecutive nights, and long commutes after shifts. Caffeine intake is modest and alcohol rare. She denies missed doses but acknowledges occasional delayed evening doses when finishing late. No new intercurrent illness. 

We discussed sleep hygiene and stabilising pre- and post-shift routines. She will trial a consistent wind-down period, blue-light reduction, and protected sleep blocks after nights. From a pharmacological standpoint, options were outlined including cautious up-titration of levetiracetam or switch/add-on to valproate or lamotrigine; given teratogenic considerations with valproate, she prefers to avoid it. For now, we agreed on a pragmatic step: adjust timing of the evening levetiracetam dose so it is taken 30–60 minutes before intended sleep on both day and night shifts, maintaining 12-hour spacing as feasible. If weekly myoclonic clusters persist over the next 6–8 weeks, we will consider increasing levetiracetam to 1.25 g twice daily. 

Safety was revisited: she understands the DVLA guidance for seizures and will refrain from driving until seizure-free according to regulations. She also has an agreed workplace plan to avoid working at unprotected heights, operating heavy machinery, or sole night shifts without recovery days. 

Investigations: Prior EEG demonstrated generalised spike-and-wave discharges consistent with generalised epilepsy; MRI brain normal. No change in neurological examination today. 

Plan: 
- Maintain levetiracetam 1 g twice daily; optimise dose timing relative to sleep.  
- Keep a seizure and sleep diary noting shift times; she will email copies before review.  
- Liaise with occupational health regarding more balanced rota if feasible.  
- Consider dose increase to 1.25 g twice daily if events remain every week after 6–8 weeks despite sleep/routine optimisation.  
- Rescue plan not required for brief myoclonus; advise ED attendance for any prolonged or clustered generalised tonic–clonic seizures. 

Follow-up in three months, sooner if she experiences further generalised tonic–clonic seizures or any adverse effects. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Overall, she reports events every week, usually brief myoclonic jerks on awakening after short or fragmented sleep.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes ongoing seizures. It states she has had a single generalised tonic–clonic seizure in the last two months, but more importantly: "Overall, she reports events every week, usually brief myoclonic jerks on awakening after short or fragmented sleep." Per the rule to select the highest frequency, weekly myoclonic events are more frequent than a single seizure over two months. Therefore, the seizure frequency is 1 or more per week. Since the letter says "events every week" without a specific count, we normalize this to 1 per week as the minimal explicit weekly frequency.
seizure_frequency_number: Overall, she reports events every week, usually brief myoclonic jerks on awakening after short or fragmented sleep.
